mnemonics.title: Array Indexing
mnemonics.content: A-R-R-A-Y: Always Remember - Read Array's Index Yourself. First element at index 0, last at n-1.

mnemonics.title: Array Operations Complexity
mnemonics.content: ACID: Access (O(1)), Create (O(n)), Insert (O(n)), Delete (O(n)) - except at ends.

mnemonics.title: Row vs Column Major
mnemonics.content: R-C: Row = Rows stored first, like reading a book left-to-right. Column = Columns stacked vertically.

mnemonics.title: Static vs Dynamic Arrays
mnemonics.content: SAD: Static Array Doesn't resize. Dynamic Array Resizes.

acronyms.expansion: Last In First Out - Not arrays but related to stack implementation using arrays
acronyms.usage: Stacks can be implemented using arrays where push/pop operations occur at one end.

acronyms.expansion: First In First Out - Used in queue implementations
acronyms.usage: Queues can be implemented using circular arrays for efficient enqueue/dequeue operations.

acronyms.expansion: Locally Optimal Be Suboptimal - Array search strategies
acronyms.usage: For small arrays, linear search can be faster than binary search due to less overhead.
Arrays leverage RAM's random access capability for O(1) element retrieval.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a row of numbered mailboxes in a post office. Each mailbox (element) is next to each other. To find mailbox #5, you just count 5 from the start.
Think of a chess board (8×8). Rows are horizontal (rank), columns are vertical (file). Element [2][3] is at row 2, column 3.
A measuring ruler: The first mark is at position 0, not 1. Each subsequent mark represents the offset from the start.
Imagine a hotel with expanding rooms. When full, you move to a bigger hotel (2x size), copying all guests (elements) to the new location.

analogies.analogy: Like a parking lot with numbered spaces. Each car (element) occupies one space, all spaces are next to each other, and you can directly go to space #7 without checking spaces 1-6.
Like floor numbers in buildings. In some countries, ground floor is floor 0, first floor is floor 1. Array indices work similarly - first element is index 0.
Like a fixed number of seats in a theater. Once all seats are filled, no one else can enter until someone leaves (but seat count doesn't change).
Like a rubber band that stretches. Initially small, but when stretched to capacity, it snaps to a bigger size. The content remains the same but occupies more space.
